title: The jar where I drop one coin a day
summary: Reflect on the small daily habit of saving one coin in a jar and what the slow weight of it taught you.
topic: A tiny daily saving habit that grew slowly into something real
prompt: Write 9–11 sentences reflecting on a small daily saving habit (a coin in a jar, a note in a notebook). Describe the habit, how it grew, and what it taught you about patience. Use present simple for the habit, past simple for results, and comparatives.
hintStarters: Every evening, when I empty my pockets, I drop one coin into…
The jar fills up so slowly that for weeks it looks almost…
I started this habit because I wanted to learn how to…
When I finally counted the coins last month, I was surprised that…
sampleText: Every evening, when I empty my pockets at home in Nairobi, I drop one coin into an old glass jar by the door. It is a small habit, and at first it felt almost silly. One coin is worth very little, and the jar fills up so slowly that for weeks it looks almost the same. I started this habit two years ago because I wanted to learn how to be patient with money. Some evenings I was tempted to spend the coin instead, but I kept the rule simple: one coin, every day, no excuses. On hard days, dropping that single coin reminded me that I still had a little to give to the future. The jar grew heavier month by month, though I never really watched it. When I finally counted the coins last month, I was surprised. It was much more than I expected, enough to fix my mother's roof before the rains. The money mattered, but the lesson mattered more. Small amounts feel like nothing on any single day, yet they are stronger than they look over time. The habit changed how I think: I no longer wait until I have a lot before I begin. Now I trust the slow weight of small things, because that jar proved it to me.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Comparatives for slow change
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng so sánh hơn để cho thấy sự thay đổi dần: 'The jar grew heavier month by month', 'much more than I expected', và 'they are stronger than they look'. So sánh hơn giúp diễn tả sự lớn dần theo thời gian.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses comparatives to show gradual change: 'The jar grew heavier month by month', 'much more than I expected', and 'they are stronger than they look'. Comparatives help express growth over time.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: no longer for a habit that stopped
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'I no longer wait until I have a lot before I begin' dùng 'no longer' để nói một thói quen cũ đã chấm dứt. 'No longer' đứng trước động từ chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I no longer wait until I have a lot before I begin' uses 'no longer' to say that an old habit has stopped. 'No longer' goes before the main verb.

sampleTranslationVi: Mỗi tối, khi tôi trút hết túi ở nhà tại Nairobi, tôi thả một đồng xu vào một cái lọ thủy tinh cũ bên cửa. Đó là một thói quen nhỏ, và lúc đầu nó có vẻ gần như ngớ ngẩn. Một đồng xu chẳng đáng bao nhiêu, và cái lọ đầy lên rất chậm đến mức trong nhiều tuần trông nó gần như y nguyên. Tôi bắt đầu thói quen này hai năm trước vì tôi muốn học cách kiên nhẫn với tiền bạc. Vài buổi tối tôi bị cám dỗ tiêu đồng xu đó thay vì để dành, nhưng tôi giữ quy tắc đơn giản: một đồng xu, mỗi ngày, không viện cớ. Vào những ngày khó khăn, việc thả một đồng xu duy nhất đó nhắc tôi rằng tôi vẫn còn một chút để dành cho tương lai. Cái lọ nặng dần lên từng tháng, dù tôi chưa từng thật sự để ý. Khi cuối cùng tôi đếm các đồng xu vào tháng trước, tôi đã ngạc nhiên. Nó nhiều hơn nhiều so với tôi mong đợi, đủ để sửa mái nhà của mẹ tôi trước mùa mưa. Số tiền có ý nghĩa, nhưng bài học còn ý nghĩa hơn. Những khoản nhỏ có vẻ như chẳng là gì trong bất kỳ ngày đơn lẻ nào, nhưng chúng mạnh hơn vẻ bề ngoài theo thời gian. Thói quen đó đã thay đổi cách tôi suy nghĩ: tôi không còn chờ đến khi có nhiều rồi mới bắt đầu. Giờ tôi tin vào sức nặng chậm rãi của những điều nhỏ bé, vì cái lọ đó đã chứng minh điều đó với tôi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about the daily habit.
options: Every evening I drop one coin into an old glass jar every day.
Every evening I drop one coin into an old glass jar.
Every evening I drops one coin into an old glass jar.
Every evening I drop one coin in to an old glass jar.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'drop' fits the habit, with 'into' as one word. The second is redundant with two time phrases; 'drops' disagrees with 'I'; 'in to' should be 'into'.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the movement word 'into' or 'in to' here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that uses 'so … that' for the slow filling.
options: The jar fills up so slow that for weeks it looks the same.
The jar fills up too slowly that for weeks it looks the same.
The jar fills up so slowly that for weeks it looks the same.
The jar fills up so slowly what for weeks it looks the same.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So slowly that' uses the adverb and the linking 'that'. 'So slow' uses an adjective; 'too slowly that' is not the structure; 'what' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'fills up', do you need 'slow' or 'slowly'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence gives the reason for starting, using 'because'?
options: I started because of I wanted to learn how to be patient with money.
I started because I wanted learn how to be patient with money.
I started, because to learn how to be patient with money.
I started because I wanted to learn how to be patient with money.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because I wanted to learn' is correct with the infinitive. 'Because of' needs a noun; 'wanted learn' drops 'to'; the last drops the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'wanted', which form follows: 'learn' or 'to learn'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that says she felt pulled to spend it.
options: I was tempted to spend the coin instead.
I was tempted spend the coin instead.
I was tempting to spend the coin instead.
I was tempted to spending the coin instead.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tempted to spend' uses the infinitive and the '-ed' feeling form. 'Tempted spend' drops 'to'; 'tempting' describes a cause; 'to spending' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Did she feel the pull ('-ed') or cause it ('-ing')?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence with the correct comparative about the jar.
options: The jar grew more heavy month by month.
The jar grew heavier month by month.
The jar grew heavyer month by month.
The jar grew heaviest month by month.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Heavier' is the correct comparative of 'heavy'. 'More heavy' is wrong; 'heavyer' misspells it; 'heaviest' is a superlative.
mcQuestions.hint: How do you form the comparative of 'heavy'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence about the count is best written?
options: When I counted the coins last month, I was surprising.
When I count the coins last month, I was surprised.
When I counted the coins last month, I was surprised.
When I counted the coins last month, I was surprise.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I was surprised' is the feeling, with past 'counted'. 'Surprising' describes a cause; 'count' lacks past tense; 'surprise' is the noun/base verb.
mcQuestions.hint: Did she feel surprise ('-ed') or cause it ('-ing')?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence with a natural comparative of quantity.
options: It was much most than I expected.
It was much more that I expected.
It was many more than I expected.
It was much more than I expected.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Much more than' is correct for an uncountable amount of money. 'Most' is superlative; 'that' should be 'than'; 'many more' needs a countable noun.
mcQuestions.hint: For money (uncountable), is it 'much more' or 'many more'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that shows the amount was enough for a purpose.
options: It was enough to fix my mother's roof.
It was enough for fix my mother's roof.
It was enough to fixing my mother's roof.
It was enough fix my mother's roof.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Enough to + base verb' (fix) is the correct pattern. 'For fix' and 'to fixing' are wrong; the last drops 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'enough', which form follows: 'to fix' or 'fixing'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence states the main lesson naturally?
options: Small amounts are more strong than they look over time.
Small amounts are stronger than they look over time.
Small amounts are stronger as they look over time.
Small amounts are strongest than they look over time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stronger than' is the correct comparative. 'More strong' is wrong; 'as' does not follow a comparative; 'strongest' is a superlative.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the comparative form of 'strong'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence about a changed habit using 'no longer'.
options: I no longer waits until I have a lot before I begin.
I not longer wait until I have a lot before I begin.
I no longer wait until I have a lot before I begin.
I no longer waiting until I have a lot before I begin.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer wait' uses the base verb after 'no longer'. 'Waits' disagrees with 'I'; 'not longer' is wrong; 'waiting' lacks an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: Is the phrase 'no longer' or 'not longer'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why is it so hard to trust small actions that show no result for a long time? What helps people keep going before they can see the proof?
